markdown:
## Genome Size and Complexity


1. Larger genomes do **not** necessarily indicate greater biological complexity.
2. Key contributors to variation include:
  1. **Non-coding DNA:** repetitive sequences, introns, and transposable elements.
  2. **Polyploidy:** whole-genome duplication, especially common in plants.
  3. **Gene duplication and intron variation:** expansion of non-essential DNA regions.

<callout type="example">Humans (~3.2 × 10⁹ bp) have a smaller genome than some amphibians or plants (>10¹¹ bp) despite higher organismal complexity.</callout>

## Summary Table (Typical Genome Sizes)


| Group | Approximate Genome Size | Notable Feature |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Bacteria | 1–10 Mbp | Compact, mostly coding DNA |
| Fungi | 10–100 Mbp | Moderate non-coding regions |
| Plants | 100 Mbp – 100 Gbp | Polyploidy, high non-coding DNA |
| Animals | 100 Mbp – 10 Gbp | Large variation; no link to complexity |

<callout type="self_review">Why might genome size increase without added complexity?</callout>
